topic: A student learns their thesis advisor is leaving the university
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox with the stark finality of a closing door. Dr. Albright was leaving. My stomach performed a slow, nauseating flip. I reread the subject line three times, my thumb hovering over the 'reply' button before retreating. The air in my cramped dorm room suddenly felt too thick, the fluorescent light buzzing with an aggressive intensity. I stared at the blinking cursor on my screen, the words of my entire academic future suddenly feeling flimsy and unsupported. I pushed back from my desk, the cheap chair scraping loudly against the linoleum floor. I needed coffee, desperately, but the caffeine seemed to have evaporated, leaving behind a hollow, trembling energy.

[story 2]

I found myself pacing the length of the university quad, the late afternoon sun casting long, judgmental shadows across the manicured lawns. Every time I stopped, I’d check my phone, half-expecting a message from Dr. Albright, a sign that this entire situation was a cruel joke. My hands felt clammy, slick with nervous sweat despite the cool autumn air. I stopped near the fountain, watching the water splash in predictable, rhythmic arcs. A knot tightened in my chest, a heavy, immovable pressure that made it hard to draw a full breath. *What now?* The question echoed in the sudden silence of my own head, unanswered and vast.

[story 3]

When I finally got to the departmental office, the silence was deafening. Dr. Albright wasn't there, which was the first shock. The empty chair at the corner desk seemed to mock me. I stood by the window, watching students hurry past, their conversations bright and uncomplicated. My gaze kept snagging on the framed photographs on the wall—pictures of Dr. Albright laughing, looking engaged, alive. A strange, cold detachment settled over me, as if I were observing a scene from a film I didn't understand. I slowly turned away from the window, my shoulders slumping forward, and the weight of the impending unknown settled squarely on my chest, making every movement feel labored.
